organic: Remove and burn all fallen leaves and infected fruit immediately
Spray neem oil solution: 3 tablespoons neem oil + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, every 7 days
Apply compost tea spray: dilute 1 cup compost in 4 litres of water, strain and spray on leaves weekly
Dust wood ash lightly over leaves and around the base after rain
prevention: Rake and destroy fallen leaves every season to remove overwintering spores
Prune branches to ensure good airflow — keep canopy open
Water at the base only, never overhead, to keep leaves dry
Plant resistant apple varieties when replacing old trees
Apple___Apple_scab.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, compost, wood ash, water

fungicides: Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from bud break
Copper oxychloride 50WP — mix 3g per litre of water, spray before and after rain
Carbendazim 50WP (Bavistin) — mix 1g per litre of water, spray at pink bud stage and petal fall
inorganic.application_timing: Early morning or late evening; avoid spraying in direct sun or strong wind
inorganic.safety_warning: Always wear gloves, mask and eye protection. Keep children and animals away for 24 hours after spraying. Do not spray near water sources or beehives.

organic: Cut out all infected branches 15cm below the visible rot and burn them
Mix 3 tablespoons neem oil + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, spray every 7 days
Apply copper sulfate solution: 1 teaspoon per litre of water, spray before and after flowering
Remove all mummified fruit hanging on branches — they harbour the fungus
prevention: Prune dead and weak branches every winter before buds open
Remove all fallen fruit and leaves from the ground promptly
Avoid wounding tree bark during farm work — wounds are entry points
Space trees at least 3 metres apart for good air circulation
Apple___Black_rot.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, copper sulfate, water

fungicides: Captan 50WP — mix 2g per litre of water, spray every 10-14 days from bud break to harvest
Thiophanate-methyl 70WP (Topsin-M) — mix 1g per litre of water, apply at petal fall and repeat after 14 days
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days
inorganic.application_timing: Apply before rain events when possible; repeat after heavy rain
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, full-sleeve clothing and a mask. Wash hands and face after handling. Store chemicals in original containers away from food.

organic: Remove and burn all orange-spotted leaves as soon as they appear
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, every 7 days during spring
Apply baking soda solution: 1 teaspoon per litre of water, spray in the evening every 5 days
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water at bud break and after petal fall
prevention: Remove any nearby cedar or juniper trees within 100 metres if possible
Rake and destroy all fallen leaves at the end of every season
Do not water leaves — irrigate at the base only
Choose rust-resistant apple varieties for new plantings
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, baking soda, copper sulfate, water

fungicides: Myclobutanil 10WP (Rally) — mix 1g per litre of water, spray at pink bud stage, petal fall, and 7-10 days after
Propiconazole 25EC (Tilt) — mix 1ml per litre of water, apply at bud break and repeat every 14 days
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, protective spray every 7-10 days in spring
inorganic.application_timing: Critical to apply during spring bud break before spores release; early morning preferred
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, mask and protective clothing. Do not spray near water sources. Keep spray away from eyes and skin.

prevention: Water regularly at the base, never wetting the leaves
Add compost around the base every 4 weeks during the growing season
Check leaves and fruit weekly for early signs of spots or rot
Prune for open canopy each year to maintain good airflow
Water at the base only, keeping leaves dry to prevent fungal issues
Mulch with 5cm of compost around the base to retain moisture
Check plants weekly for unusual spots, wilting, or discolouration
Prune dead stems every spring before new growth starts

organic: Remove all leaves with white powdery coating and burn them away from the farm
Spray baking soda solution: 1 tablespoon per litre of water + 1 teaspoon dish soap, every 5 days
Apply neem oil: 2 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, spray in the evening
Dust wood ash over affected leaves in the early morning — reapply after rain
prevention: Prune dense branches to allow sunlight and airflow through the canopy
Avoid overhead watering — water at the base only
Do not over-fertilise with nitrogen — excess nitrogen promotes soft growth that mildew attacks
Remove fallen leaves promptly at the end of each season
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.local_materials: baking soda, dish soap, neem oil, wood ash, water

fungicides: Wettable sulfur 80WP — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days when humidity is high
Myclobutanil 10WP (Rally) — mix 1g per litre of water, apply at first sign of white powder and repeat after 14 days
Tebuconazole 25EC (Folicur) — mix 1ml per litre of water, spray every 14 days
inorganic.application_timing: Early morning when temperature is below 30 degrees C; avoid spraying sulfur when temperatures exceed 32 degrees C
inorganic.safety_warning: Sulfur can cause skin and eye irritation — wear full protective gear. Do not mix sulfur with other pesticides. Keep children away for 48 hours.

prevention: Water at the base; never wet the leaves or fruit
Add compost around the base every 3 weeks during the growing season
Inspect leaves and shoots weekly for white coatings or spots
Thin crowded branches each year to maintain good airflow

organic: Remove and burn heavily infected lower leaves at the first sign of long grey lesions
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, every 7 days
Apply compost tea spray: 1 cup compost steeped in 4 litres of water overnight, strain and spray weekly
Avoid overhead irrigation — switch to furrow or drip irrigation at the base
prevention: Rotate maize with legumes or other non-host crops each season
Leave at least 30cm between rows for good air circulation
Till crop residues into soil after harvest to reduce fungal spores
Plant varieties with moderate resistance to grey leaf spot where available
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, compost, water

fungicides: Azoxystrobin 23SC (Amistar) — mix 1ml per litre of water, apply at first sign of lesions and repeat after 14 days
Propiconazole 25EC (Tilt) — mix 1ml per litre of water, spray from flag leaf stage every 14 days
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, spray preventively every 10-14 days
inorganic.application_timing: Apply in early morning or evening; use knapsack sprayer to reach lower leaves
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, mask and protective clothing. Chlorothalonil is a strong irritant — avoid inhaling spray mist. Do not enter the field for 12 hours.

organic: Remove and destroy leaves with reddish-brown pustules as soon as they appear
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, every 7 days
Dust wood ash over leaves early in the morning to discourage spore germination
Apply garlic spray: blend 10 garlic cloves in 1 litre of water, strain, dilute to 4 litres, spray weekly
prevention: Plant rust-resistant maize varieties whenever available in your area
Rotate maize fields every season — never plant maize in the same plot twice in a row
Avoid dense planting — space rows at least 30cm apart
Destroy all crop residue after harvest to remove overwintering spores
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, wood ash, garlic, water

fungicides: Propiconazole 25EC (Tilt) — mix 1ml per litre of water, spray at first rust pustule appearance; repeat after 14 days
Azoxystrobin 23SC (Amistar) — mix 1ml per litre of water, preventive spray from tasseling stage
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days as protection
inorganic.application_timing: Best applied in the early morning before dew dries; high volume spray for good canopy penetration
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves and a mask when mixing and spraying. Wash spray equipment thoroughly after use. Keep chemicals locked away from children.

organic: Cut off and burn leaves showing long cigar-shaped grey-green lesions
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Apply copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water, spray at first sign of symptoms
Spray compost tea: steep 1 cup compost in 4 litres water for 24 hours, strain and apply weekly
prevention: Rotate maize with soybeans or groundnuts every season
Plough crop debris deep into the soil after harvest
Space plants at least 25cm apart within rows to reduce humidity
Select blight-tolerant maize varieties when purchasing seed
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, copper sulfate, compost, water

fungicides: Azoxystrobin 23SC (Amistar) — mix 1ml per litre of water, spray at flag leaf emergence; repeat after 14 days
Propiconazole 25EC (Tilt) — mix 1ml per litre of water, apply at first sign of grey-green lesions
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, preventive spray every 10 days during humid weather
inorganic.application_timing: Apply early morning; ensure good coverage of all leaf surfaces including lower canopy
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear full protective clothing including mask and gloves. Avoid breathing spray mist. Wash hands and face thoroughly after application.

prevention: Water at the base of plants; avoid wetting leaves
Add compost around the base every 3 weeks for strong growth
Walk through the field weekly and check for early lesions or rust pustules
Rotate with a different crop family each season

organic: Remove all infected berries, leaves and mummified fruit and burn them
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water every 7 days from bud break through harvest
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, every 7 days
Remove all crop debris from the ground at end of season and bury or burn it
prevention: Train vines on a trellis to keep fruit and leaves off the ground
Prune heavily each winter to open the canopy and improve airflow
Water at the base only — overhead watering spreads spores rapidly
Sanitise pruning tools with diluted dish soap between cuts
Grape___Black_rot.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

fungicides: Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from bud break to harvest
Captan 50WP — mix 2g per litre of water, apply before and after rain events
Myclobutanil 10WP (Rally) — mix 1g per litre of water, apply at early shoot growth and repeat every 10-14 days
inorganic.application_timing: Begin spraying at bud break in spring; timing is critical — apply before infection periods (rain + warm weather)
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, mask and eye protection. Avoid inhaling Mancozeb dust when mixing. Do not apply within 7 days of harvest.

organic: Prune out all wood showing dark internal streaking and burn it immediately
Paint pruning wounds with a thick paste of wood ash mixed with water to seal cuts
Apply neem oil spray: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water monthly
Avoid any wounding of the vine during the wet season when fungi spread most easily
prevention: Prune only during dry weather and seal all large cuts immediately
Sanitise pruning shears with dish soap solution between every vine
Remove and burn severely infected vines to protect neighbouring plants
Maintain good soil drainage around vine roots — waterlogged roots worsen esca
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, wood ash, water

fungicides: Thiophanate-methyl 70WP (Topsin-M) — mix 1g per litre of water, apply as wound protectant immediately after pruning
Copper oxychloride 50WP — mix 3g per litre of water, spray over pruning cuts and the whole vine in early spring
inorganic.application_timing: Apply immediately after pruning to seal wounds; spray in dry weather to allow absorption
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves and mask when applying. Thiophanate-methyl is absorbed through skin — avoid prolonged contact. Keep spray away from water sources.

organic: Remove and burn all leaves showing dark angular spots immediately
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Apply baking soda solution: 1 tablespoon per litre of water, spray in the evening every 5 days
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water at the first sign of symptoms
prevention: Trellis vines to keep leaves elevated and allow good airflow
Remove fallen leaves from around the base promptly
Water only at the base; never overhead
Open up dense canopy by pruning each winter
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, baking soda, copper sulfate, water

fungicides: Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days starting at berry set
Copper oxychloride 50WP — mix 3g per litre of water, apply preventively before expected rain
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, spray every 10-14 days
inorganic.application_timing: Early morning; ensure thorough coverage of both sides of leaves
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear full protective gear. Do not spray near rivers or ponds. Wash all equipment thoroughly after use.

prevention: Water at the base only; wet leaves encourage fungal disease
Prune yearly to keep canopy open and reduce humidity inside the vine
Check leaves and berries every week for early spots or discolouration
Remove fallen leaves from the ground each season to reduce spore load

organic: Remove and burn all branches and trees showing yellowing, mottled leaves and small lopsided fruit — there is no cure
Apply neem oil spray: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days to reduce psyllid insect vectors
Spray garlic extract: blend 10 cloves in 1 litre water, strain, dilute to 4 litres, spray weekly to repel psyllids
Add compost 5cm deep around the base to strengthen tree immunity and root health
prevention: Never move budwood or plant material from infected areas to healthy farms
Inspect trees weekly for the Asian citrus psyllid insect — the sole carrier of HLB
Remove infected trees immediately to prevent spread to healthy trees
Buy certified disease-free seedlings from trusted nurseries only
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, garlic, compost, water

pesticides: Imidacloprid 17.8SL (Confidor) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray every 21 days to control Asian citrus psyllid vector
Thiamethoxam 25WG (Actara) — mix 0.5g per litre of water, spray at flush emergence when psyllids are active
insecticides: Imidacloprid 17.8SL (Confidor) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray on new flush growth every 21 days
Dimethoate 30EC — mix 1.5ml per litre of water, spray every 14 days during flush growth to control psyllids
inorganic.application_timing: Spray when new leaf flush emerges (every 6-8 weeks); early morning to protect beneficial insects
inorganic.safety_warning: Imidacloprid is highly toxic to bees — do not spray on flowering trees. Wear gloves, mask and goggles. Do not allow children or animals near treated trees for 48 hours.

organic: Remove and burn all infected leaves, shoots and fruit showing water-soaked spots
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water, apply every 7 days starting at bud swell
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Avoid any pruning or farm work during wet or windy weather when bacteria spread
prevention: Water at the base only — wet leaves and fruit are rapidly infected
Prune to keep the canopy open and reduce humidity around leaves
Plant windbreaks around the orchard — strong winds carry the bacteria
Sanitise pruning tools between each tree with dish soap solution
Peach___Bacterial_spot.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

pesticides: Copper hydroxide 77WP (Kocide) — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from bud swell through harvest
Oxytetracycline 17WP (Mycoshield) — mix 1g per litre of water, apply at petal fall and twice more at 10-day intervals
inorganic.application_timing: Apply at bud swell in spring before symptoms appear; repeat after rain
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves and mask when mixing copper products — copper dust is a lung irritant. Do not spray near fish ponds. Wash hands thoroughly after use.

prevention: Water at the base; never wet the leaves or fruit
Add 5cm of compost around the base every month during the growing season
Walk through the orchard weekly and check for spots, cracking or wilting
Prune dead or crowded branches each winter to improve airflow

organic: Remove and burn all leaves and fruit with water-soaked or raised dark spots
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water every 7 days
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet — this spreads bacteria rapidly
prevention: Use only certified disease-free seed or seedlings from trusted suppliers
Rotate peppers with non-solanaceous crops every season
Water at the base; drip irrigation is strongly preferred over overhead watering
Space plants at least 45cm apart to allow air circulation and reduce humidity
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

pesticides: Copper hydroxide 77WP (Kocide) — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from transplant
Streptomycin sulfate 9SP (Agrimycin) — mix 1g per litre of water, spray at first sign of water-soaked spots; max 3 applications per season
Kasugamycin 3SL (Kasumin) — mix 2ml per litre of water, spray every 7 days during wet weather
inorganic.application_timing: Apply early morning; avoid spraying when rain is expected within 4 hours
inorganic.safety_warning: Streptomycin overuse causes resistance — limit to 3 applications per season. Wear gloves and mask. Keep children away for 24 hours.

prevention: Water at the base only and avoid wetting leaves
Add compost around the base every 3 weeks during the growing season
Inspect plants weekly for dark spots, lesions or unusual discolouration
Rotate peppers with a different crop family each season

organic: Remove and burn all leaves showing dark brown concentric ring spots immediately
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Apply baking soda solution: 1 tablespoon per litre of water, spray in the evening every 5 days
Dust wood ash lightly over leaves early in the morning after removing infected material

Water at the base in the early morning; never wet leaves in the evening
Space plants at least 40cm apart and mound soil around stems for airflow
Destroy all crop debris after harvest — early blight overwinters in old plant material
Potato___Early_blight.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, baking soda, wood ash, water

fungicides: Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from canopy closure
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, preventive spray every 7 days
Azoxystrobin 23SC (Amistar) — mix 1ml per litre of water, apply at first sign and repeat after 14 days
inorganic.application_timing: Start preventive sprays 4-6 weeks after planting; spray in early morning for best results
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, mask and long sleeves. Chlorothalonil can cause skin sensitisation — avoid repeated skin contact. Keep spray away from water bodies.

organic: Remove and burn all infected plants immediately — do not compost them
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water every 5 days at the first sign of symptoms
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 5 days
Hill soil high around plant stems to protect developing tubers from infected runoff
prevention: Plant only certified disease-free seed potatoes
Rotate potatoes with cereals or legumes — never repeat potatoes in the same field
Avoid overhead irrigation; water only at the base in the morning
Harvest promptly when tops die back — leaving tubers in wet soil causes rot
Potato___Late_blight.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

fungicides: Metalaxyl+Mancozeb 72WP (Ridomil Gold) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7 days at first sign; do not use more than 3 times per season
Cymoxanil+Mancozeb 72WP (Curzate) — mix 2g per litre of water, apply every 7-10 days when conditions favour blight
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, protective spray every 7 days in humid weather
inorganic.application_timing: Act immediately at first symptom — late blight spreads rapidly; spray in early morning or evening
inorganic.safety_warning: Ridomil resistance is common — rotate with other fungicides. Wear full protective gear. Do not spray within 7 days of harvest.

prevention: Water at the base in the morning; never let leaves stay wet overnight
Hill soil around stems every 2 weeks to support tuber development
Walk the field weekly and check leaves for dark spots or water-soaked lesions
Rotate with a non-solanaceous crop every season
Water at the base only; wet canes are prone to fungal disease
Add 5cm of compost mulch around the base each spring
Prune out old fruited canes each year to encourage strong new growth
Inspect leaves and canes weekly for early spots, rust or mildew

organic: Remove and burn all infected tillers showing water-soaked yellow streaks from leaf tips
Drain the field completely for 3 to 5 days — the bacteria thrive in standing water
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water on the remaining healthy crop every 7 days
Apply garlic extract: crush 10 garlic cloves in 1 litre of water, strain, dilute to 4 litres, spray every 5 days
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed — soak seed in 1 teaspoon copper sulfate per litre of water for 8 hours before planting
Avoid excess nitrogen fertiliser — it promotes lush soft growth that bacteria attack
Maintain good drainage in paddy fields; do not let water stagnate for long periods
Rotate rice with pulses or vegetables every second season to break the disease cycle

pesticides: Copper hydroxide 77WP (Kocide) — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days on infected tillers
Streptomycin sulfate 9SP (Agrimycin) — mix 1g per litre of water, spray at first sign of yellow streaks; max 2 applications
Kasugamycin 3SL (Kasumin) — mix 2ml per litre of water, spray every 7 days during tillering and panicle initiation
inorganic.application_timing: Spray in early morning; ensure thorough coverage of all tillers including water surface where bacteria persist
inorganic.safety_warning: Limit streptomycin to 2 sprays per season to prevent resistance. Wear gloves and mask. Do not contaminate irrigation water used by other farms downstream.

organic: Remove and burn heavily spotted leaves to reduce spore load in the field
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Apply wood ash: broadcast 50kg per acre on the soil around plants to improve potassium and reduce fungal pressure
Spray compost tea: steep 2 cups of compost in 4 litres water for 24 hours, strain and spray every 7 days
prevention: Treat seeds before planting: soak in warm water (52°C) for 10 minutes or in salt water (1 cup salt per 4 litres) to discard weak seed
Ensure adequate potassium in soil by adding wood ash or compost before transplanting
Maintain proper water depth in paddy — 5cm standing water is optimal; avoid drought stress
Rotate rice with legumes every second season to restore soil nutrients that reduce brown spot severity
Rice___Brown_spot.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, wood ash, compost, water

fungicides: Propiconazole 25EC (Tilt) — mix 1ml per litre of water, spray at booting and heading stages
Tricyclazole 75WP (Beam) — mix 0.6g per litre of water, apply at first sign of brown oval spots; effective also against blast
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, protective spray from tillering stage every 10-14 days
inorganic.application_timing: Apply at booting stage for maximum protection; spray early morning to reduce evaporation loss
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear rubber gloves and a mask when spraying paddy fields. Avoid walking in sprayed fields for 12 hours. Protect skin from prolonged exposure to Propiconazole.

organic: Remove infected tillers showing black powdery masses on leaves and burn them away from the field
Treat seed before planting: soak in 1 teaspoon copper sulfate per litre of water for 8 hours, rinse and dry
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days during tillering
Apply wood ash broadcast at 50kg per acre to reduce soil fungal load before transplanting
prevention: Use certified clean seed from a trusted government or NGO seed supplier
Avoid waterlogging — maintain proper drainage channels around paddy bunds
Do not reuse seed from infected crops — smut spreads through infected seed
Rotate rice with vegetables or pulses every second season to break the smut cycle
Rice___Leaf_smut.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, wood ash, water

fungicides: Carbendazim 50WP (Bavistin) — mix 1g per litre of water, seed treatment: soak seed for 8 hours before planting; foliar spray at tillering
Tricyclazole 75WP (Beam) — mix 0.6g per litre of water, spray at tillering and booting stages
Propiconazole 25EC (Tilt) — mix 1ml per litre of water, apply at first sign of black powdery lesions
inorganic.application_timing: Seed treatment is most effective; foliar spray should begin at early tillering stage
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves when doing seed treatment. Do not allow treated seed to be eaten by animals or birds. Apply foliar sprays in early morning.

prevention: Inoculate seed with rhizobium bacteria before planting to fix nitrogen naturally
Water at the base; avoid wetting leaves especially in humid weather
Walk rows weekly and check for yellowing, lesions or pest damage
Rotate soybeans with maize or sorghum each season

organic: Remove all leaves covered in white powdery coating and burn them — never compost them
Spray baking soda solution: 1 tablespoon per litre of water + 1 teaspoon dish soap, every 5 days
Apply neem oil: 2 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, spray in the evening every 7 days
Dust wood ash lightly over infected leaves in the early morning — reapply after rain
prevention: Space plants at least 60cm apart to allow good airflow between vines
Train vines on a trellis or bamboo frame to lift leaves off the ground
Avoid overhead watering — water only at the base in the morning
Remove old vines and leaves at the end of the season to eliminate overwintering spores
Squash___Powdery_mildew.local_materials: baking soda, dish soap, neem oil, wood ash, water

fungicides: Wettable sulfur 80WP — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days; do not apply when temperature exceeds 32 degrees C

organic: Remove and burn all leaves showing small dark purple spots with scorched brown edges

Rotate strawberry beds every 3 years and avoid replanting in soil that had scorch
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, copper sulfate, water

fungicides: Captan 50WP — mix 2g per litre of water, spray every 10-14 days from early spring through harvest

Inspect leaves weekly for dark spots or brown margins

organic: Remove and burn all leaves and fruit with water-soaked dark spots immediately

Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days

Tomato___Early_blight.local_materials: neem oil, wood ash, baking soda, dish soap, water

fungicides: Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from first symptom
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, preventive spray every 7 days in humid weather

inorganic.application_timing: Apply in early morning or evening; ensure good coverage of lower leaves where infection starts
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, mask and long-sleeve clothing. Mancozeb can cause skin sensitisation with repeated exposure. Do not spray within 7 days of harvest.

organic: Remove and burn all infected plants immediately — never leave them in the field
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water every 5 days at first sign of symptoms
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 5 days
Avoid working in the field during rain or heavy dew — this spreads the disease rapidly
prevention: Never plant tomatoes near potatoes — late blight crosses between both crops
Water at the base in the morning only; leaves must be dry by evening
Remove and destroy all plant debris at the end of every season
Stake plants so leaves are off the ground and get good airflow
Tomato___Late_blight.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

fungicides: Metalaxyl+Mancozeb 72WP (Ridomil Gold) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7 days; limit to 3 applications per season
Cymoxanil+Mancozeb 72WP (Curzate) — mix 2g per litre of water, apply every 7-10 days during cool wet weather
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, protective spray every 5-7 days
inorganic.application_timing: Act immediately — late blight can destroy a crop within days; spray in early morning or evening
inorganic.safety_warning: Rotate fungicides to prevent Metalaxyl resistance. Wear full protective gear. Do not spray within 7 days of harvest. Dispose of empty containers safely.

organic: Remove leaves with yellow patches above and olive-green mould below and burn them
Spray baking soda: 1 tablespoon per litre of water + 1 teaspoon dish soap every 5 days
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Increase airflow by removing lower leaves and staking plants taller
prevention: Avoid growing tomatoes in humid enclosed spaces — leaf mold thrives in high humidity
Water at the base only and never in the evening
Space plants at least 50cm apart and stake all branches
Remove all plant debris after harvest and till the soil deeply
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.local_materials: baking soda, dish soap, neem oil, water

fungicides: Copper oxychloride 50WP — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days when humidity is high
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, preventive spray every 7-10 days
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, apply at first sign of yellow patches on leaves
inorganic.application_timing: Apply in early morning; spray inside greenhouse carefully to avoid chemical buildup
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves and mask, especially in enclosed greenhouse environments where spray concentration builds up. Ventilate after spraying.

organic: Remove all lower leaves showing small spots with dark borders and burn them immediately
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water every 7 days
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Mulch heavily around the base with 5cm of straw to prevent soil spores splashing onto lower leaves
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with a non-solanaceous crop each season
Stake plants to keep foliage off the ground and allow airflow
Water at the base only — overhead irrigation spreads Septoria spores rapidly
Remove all dead leaves and crop debris at the end of the season
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

fungicides: Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days from first symptom
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, protective spray every 7 days
Copper oxychloride 50WP — mix 3g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days as an alternative
inorganic.application_timing: Start spraying at first white-centred spots on lower leaves; early morning application preferred
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear gloves, mask and protective clothing. Chlorothalonil is a strong irritant — minimise inhalation and skin contact. Store locked away from food.

organic: Spray leaves forcefully with plain water to knock mites off — repeat daily for 5 days
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water, spray every 3 days
Spray garlic chili extract: blend 5 garlic cloves + 2 chilies in 1 litre water, strain, dilute to 4 litres, spray every 3 days
Dust wood ash over leaves in the early morning — mites dislike dry alkaline surfaces
prevention: Keep plants well-watered — stressed dry plants are most vulnerable to mite attacks
Avoid dusty conditions around the field; dust coats leaves and harbours mites
Inspect the underside of leaves weekly for tiny dots or webbing
Remove heavily infested leaves promptly before mites spread to the whole plant
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, garlic, chili, wood ash, water

insecticides: Abamectin 1.8EC (Vertimec) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray focusing on leaf undersides; repeat after 7 days
Spiromesifen 22.9SC (Oberon) — mix 1ml per litre of water, spray thoroughly on all leaf surfaces every 7-10 days
Propargite 57EC (Omite) — mix 2ml per litre of water, spray in early morning every 7 days
inorganic.application_timing: Spray in early morning focusing on the undersides of leaves where mites live and breed
inorganic.safety_warning: Abamectin is highly toxic to bees and aquatic organisms — do not spray near water or flowering plants. Wear gloves, mask and protective clothing. Do not re-enter field for 12 hours.

organic: Remove and burn all leaves with dark concentric ring spots — start from the bottom of the plant
Spray copper sulfate: 1 teaspoon per litre of water every 7 days
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days
Mulch with 5cm of straw or dry grass around the base to prevent soil-splash spreading spores
prevention: Stake plants and remove lower leaves to keep foliage off the ground
Water at the base only in the morning
Rotate tomatoes with a different crop family each season
Remove all crop debris after harvest and till the soil to bury remaining spores
Tomato___Target_Spot.local_materials: copper sulfate, neem oil, dish soap, water

fungicides: Azoxystrobin 23SC (Amistar) — mix 1ml per litre of water, apply at first sign of concentric ring spots; repeat after 14 days
Chlorothalonil 75WP (Daconil) — mix 2g per litre of water, preventive spray every 7-10 days
Mancozeb 75WP (Dithane M-45) — mix 2.5g per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days
inorganic.application_timing: Apply in early morning; ensure good spray coverage from bottom to top of plant canopy
inorganic.safety_warning: Wear full protective clothing when applying. Rotate fungicide classes to prevent resistance. Do not spray within 7 days of harvest.

organic: Remove and burn all plants showing yellowed, curled leaves — there is no cure once infected
Spray neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 5 days to kill whitefly vectors
Apply garlic-chili spray: blend 5 garlic cloves + 3 chilies in 1 litre water, strain, dilute to 4 litres, spray every 5 days
Place yellow sticky traps (coated with grease or neem oil) around the field to catch whiteflies
prevention: Control whitefly populations from the start of the season — they are the sole carriers of this virus
Use reflective mulch around the base of plants to confuse and repel whiteflies
Plant tomatoes away from other solanaceous crops that may harbour whiteflies
Remove and burn infected plants immediately to prevent whiteflies picking up and spreading the virus
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.local_materials: neem oil, dish soap, garlic, chili, water

pesticides: Imidacloprid 17.8SL (Confidor) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray every 14-21 days to control whitefly vector
Thiamethoxam 25WG (Actara) — mix 0.5g per litre of water, spray at transplanting and repeat every 21 days
insecticides: Imidacloprid 17.8SL (Confidor) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray every 14-21 days to control whitefly
Thiamethoxam 25WG (Actara) — mix 0.5g per litre of water, foliar or soil drench at transplant stage
Spinosad 45SC (Success) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray every 7-10 days as a safer alternative
inorganic.application_timing: Start insecticide programme at transplanting; spray early morning to avoid harming pollinators
inorganic.safety_warning: Imidacloprid is highly toxic to bees — never apply on flowering plants. Wear gloves, goggles and mask. Do not spray near beehives. Keep children away for 48 hours.

organic: Remove and burn all plants with mottled yellow-green mosaic patterns on leaves immediately
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before and after handling any plant
Disinfect all tools between plants by dipping in a solution of 1 tablespoon dish soap per litre of water
Apply neem oil: 3 tablespoons + 1 teaspoon dish soap in 4 litres of water every 7 days to reduce aphid vectors
prevention: Use certified virus-free seed — do not save seed from any plant that shows mosaicing
Never touch plants after handling tobacco products — tobacco mosaic virus can infect tomatoes
Control aphids early using neem oil spray — aphids spread mosaic viruses between plants
Remove weeds around the field that may carry the virus as reservoir hosts

pesticides: Imidacloprid 17.8SL (Confidor) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray every 14 days to control aphid and whitefly vectors
Mineral oil (white oil) 3% solution — mix 30ml per litre of water, spray to reduce aphid feeding and virus spread
insecticides: Imidacloprid 17.8SL (Confidor) — mix 0.5ml per litre of water, spray every 14 days to control aphid vectors
Acetamiprid 20SP (Mospilan) — mix 0.5g per litre of water, spray at first aphid sighting and repeat after 10 days
inorganic.application_timing: Begin vector control early — spray at transplanting and continue every 14 days throughout the season
inorganic.safety_warning: No chemical can cure mosaic virus — insecticides only prevent further spread by vectors. Wear gloves and mask. Keep spray away from eyes. Do not apply near water sources.

prevention: Water regularly at the base, not on leaves
Add compost around the base every 3 weeks
Check leaves weekly for early signs of disease
